solution: It is possible only inside the nucleus and not otherwise as it can not decay to a state of greater mass. Inside a nucleus however, the situation is different, as the binding energy of a nucleus provides the necessary energy to compensate for the neutron-proton mass difference.

subject: Physics
solution:
Binding energy is given by E=∆mc2

where ∆m is the mass defect.

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus having mass number 242 is 7.6 MeV.

The initial binding energy is,

BE=242×7.6 MeV

The energy per nucleon of the nucleus with mass number 121 is 8.1 MeV.

Therefore, binding energy is

BE'=2121×8.1 MeV

The gain in the binding energy is 

BE'-BE = (8.1 – 7.6) × 242 MeV=121 MeV

= 121 MeV

subject: Physics
options: A and B decay at the same rate always.
A and $\mathrm{B}$ decay at the same rate initially.
A will decay at a faster rate than B.
$\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$.
solution:
$\left(\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}\right)_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(\mathrm{t}_{\text {mean }}\right)_{\mathrm{B}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{0.693}{\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{A}}=0.693 \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}
$$
or $\lambda_{\mathrm{A}} < \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}$
Also rate of decay $=\lambda \mathrm{N}$
Initially number of atoms $(\mathrm{N})$ of both are equal but since $\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}$, therefore $\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$

subject: Physics
solution:
When an alpha particle is emitted from nucleus the nucleus loses two proton and two neutrons. This means the atomic mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2.

In beta decays, the mass number of the atoms remains the same, but the atomic number changes: the atomic number increases in the negative beta decay and decreases in the positive beta decay by 1, respectively.

In gamma decay, mass number and atomic number remains unchanged.

Step by step variation is given below:

 84218A⟶α 82214A1⟶β- 83214A2⟶γ 83214A3⟶α 81210A4⟶β+ 80210A5⟶γ 80210A6

subject: Physics
solution:
Nuclear radius follows $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$ where $A$ is mass number.
Given $\beta = 8\alpha$, we have $R_\alpha = R_0 \alpha^{1/3}$ and $R_\beta = R_0 (8\alpha)^{1/3} = R_0 \cdot 2 \alpha^{1/3} = 2R_\alpha$.
Thus $R_\alpha/R_\beta = 1/2 = 0.5$.

NN0=12n   ...1

where, n is the number of half lives.

Substitute 18 for NN0 into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of n.

18=12n⇒123=12n⇒n=3

The half-life of the radioactive element can be calculated as follows:

tt12=n   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) and solve to calculate the half-life.

3 dayst12= 3⇒t12= 1 day

The initial and the final mass of the radioactive element are related by

mm0=12t't12   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the initial mass of the substance.

8×10-3 kgm0= 1251⇒m0= 25×8×10-3 kg= 256 g
